question: [File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a panic by causing the Rosetta /network/list endpoint to return an empty NetworkIdentifiers slice, so that networkList.NetworkIdentifiers[0] panics and crashes the node process, causing that node to shut down and contributing to ≥30% node loss across the cluster?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a bounds check on networkList.NetworkIdentifiers[0] by crafting a response that returns a nil or zero-length NetworkIdentifiers list, causing an index-out-of-range panic that terminates the goroutine or process on multiple nodes simultaneously, meeting the ≥30% shutdown threshold?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user repeatedly call GET /health/readiness without authentication or rate limiting, causing each call to open a new PostgreSQL connection via postgres.New(postgres.Config{DSN: rosettaConfig.Db.GetDsn()}), exhausting the database connection pool and making all nodes that share the same DB report unavailable, triggering orchestrator-driven restarts of ≥30% of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that readiness checks have SkipOnErr: false for both postgresql and network checks, so that a single transient DB or network error causes the readiness endpoint to return HTTP 503, causing Kubernetes to remove the node from the load balancer and effectively shutting it down from the perspective of the network, achievable across ≥30% of nodes simultaneously?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the self-referential nature of checkNetworkStatus (which calls http://localhost:{port}/network/list on the same server) to create a recursive health-check loop that exhausts goroutines or file descriptors on each node, causing the node to become unresponsive and be removed from the network by orchestrators, affecting ≥30% of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user send a large number of concurrent GET /health/readiness requests, each of which triggers checkNetworkStatus to make an outbound HTTP call to http://localhost:{port}/network/list, causing a goroutine pile-up that exhausts memory on the node and causes it to crash, contributing to ≥30% node shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the 10-second Timeout on both readiness health checks (postgresql and network) to hold open goroutines for up to 20 seconds per request, and by sending a sustained stream of requests cause goroutine exhaustion that crashes the node process, contributing to ≥30% node shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the unauthenticated /health/readiness endpoint to trigger repeated PostgreSQL health checks, each consuming a DB connection for up to 10 seconds, exhausting the connection pool shared across nodes and causing all nodes to fail their readiness checks simultaneously, resulting in ≥30% of nodes being marked unavailable?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that rosettaClient is created with client.NewConfiguration(serverUrl, 'readiness-check', nil) with no timeout override, so that if the local Rosetta server is slow to respond, the health check goroutine blocks indefinitely, accumulating blocked goroutines that eventually exhaust the runtime and crash the node?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user cause the NetworkList call to return a response where NetworkIdentifiers is nil (not just empty), so that the nil dereference at networkList.NetworkIdentifiers[0] causes a runtime panic that crashes the node, and if this condition is reproducible across nodes, causes ≥30% node shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of rate limiting on GET /health/liveness and GET /health/readiness to send a high-frequency request storm that saturates the HTTP server's connection queue, causing legitimate traffic to be dropped and the node to appear unresponsive to orchestrators, triggering automated restarts of ≥30% of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that both readiness checks (postgresql and network) run with SkipOnErr: false, meaning a single crafted condition that causes both checks to time out simultaneously will cause the readiness endpoint to return 503, and if triggered across ≥30% of nodes, effectively removes them from the network without brute force?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus creates a new rosettaClient on every NewHealthController call but reuses it across all health check invocations, so that if the client's internal state becomes corrupted or its connection pool is exhausted by concurrent health checks, all subsequent readiness checks on that node fail, causing the node to be removed from the network?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the log.Errorf call that logs the full error from /network/list failure to inject log-forging payloads (e.g., newline characters in error messages) that corrupt log aggregation systems, causing operators to miss real alerts about node health and allowing a degraded state affecting ≥30% of nodes to go undetected?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus calls rosettaClient.NetworkAPI.NetworkStatus with the first NetworkIdentifier from the list, and if that identifier is attacker-influenced (e.g., via a MITM or DNS rebinding on localhost), cause the NetworkStatus call to fail and mark the node as not ready, contributing to ≥30% node unavailability?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the publicly accessible /health/readiness endpoint to enumerate which nodes are currently healthy vs. degraded, and then time targeted requests to coincide with maintenance windows to push ≥30% of nodes into a failed readiness state without triggering brute-force detection?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the postgres health check using postgres.New(postgres.Config{DSN: rosettaConfig.Db.GetDsn()}) to infer the database hostname, port, username, and database name from error messages returned in the health check JSON response, enabling further targeted attacks that degrade ≥30% of nodes?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the context passed to the checkNetworkStatus closure is the request context, so that by sending requests with very short deadlines (e.g., via Connection: close with immediate disconnect), the context is cancelled before the health check completes, causing the check to return an error and mark the node as not ready?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the /health/readiness endpoint's JSON response body to extract internal error details from the postgresql or network check failures, including partial DSN information or internal network topology, enabling reconnaissance that facilitates a targeted attack causing ≥30% node shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a maximum concurrent health check limit to send thousands of simultaneous GET /health/readiness requests, each spawning a goroutine that calls checkNetworkStatus and blocks for up to 10 seconds, causing memory exhaustion (OOM kill) on the node and contributing to ≥30% node shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that health.New() and health.WithChecks() are called at startup with no re-initialization logic, so that if the initial DSN or port configuration is invalid, the health controller silently returns errors that cause all readiness checks to permanently fail, marking all nodes as unavailable and causing ≥30% node shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the rosettaClient created in checkNetworkStatus uses http://localhost:{port} with no TLS, allowing a local network attacker (e.g., via shared hosting or container escape) to intercept the health check traffic and inject a response with an empty NetworkIdentifiers list, causing a panic that shuts down the node?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: Routes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the /health/liveness endpoint's unconditional HTTP 200 response to distinguish between a live-but-not-ready node and a fully healthy node, using this information to selectively target not-ready nodes with additional load that pushes them into a crashed state, contributing to ≥30% node shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: checkNetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that checkNetworkStatus makes two sequential HTTP calls (NetworkList then NetworkStatus) with no circuit breaker, so that if the first call succeeds but the second fails, the node is marked not ready, and by crafting conditions where NetworkStatus consistently fails (e.g., via resource exhaustion of the status handler), cause ≥30% of nodes to fail readiness checks?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/middleware/health.go -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: NewHealthController()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the readiness health check configuration has no jitter or backoff between check retries, so that a coordinated request storm to /health/readiness across multiple nodes causes all nodes to simultaneously attempt DB connections, exhausting the shared PostgreSQL connection limit and causing all nodes to fail their readiness checks at the same time?
